P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322·5662 
Paul H. Morgan 
City Attorney 
City of Westminster 
Administration 
•• 
Executive/legal 
322·5660 
322·5901 
June 8, 1983 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
Statements of Economic Intere~t 
322-6444 
660 Newport Center Drive, Suite 295 
Newport Beach, CA 
92660 
Dear Mr. Morgan: 
Re: 
Your Request for Written Advice 
Our No. A-83-137 
Your letter requesting our advice asks whether there is any 
potential for conflict of interest in the fact that one of your 
planning commissioners, Mr. Jack Miller, has recently purchased a 
local newspaper. 
The newspaper currently has a contract with the 
City for the publication of the City's legal notices of hearings 
before tne Planning Commission and other commissions and committees 
of the City, as well as meetings of the City Council. 
The Political Reform Act (the "Act")l/ provides that a public 
official shall not make or participate in making any governmental 
decision which will have a material financial effect on any 
financial interest of the official. 
Section 87100. 
Financial 
interest is defined to include any business entity in which the 
official has an investment wortn more than $1,000, which Mr. Miller 
presumably has in the newspaper. 
Financial interest also includes 
any source of income to Mr. Miller of $250 or more (which, if he 
owns the newspaper completely, includes any source of income to the 
1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91014. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code. 
Our advice is confined 
to the Act's provisons and does not cover other statutory or 
common law matters, such as Section 1090, et~. 


Paul H. Morgan 
June 8, 1983 
Page 2 
paper of $250 or morel/). 
Section 87103. 
Consequently, 
Mr. Miller may not maKe or participate in making any decisions 
which will materially affect such financial interests in a 
manner which is distinguishable from the decisions' effects on 
the public generally. 
Mr. Miller's decisions as a planning cornrnisioner are not 
likely to be such as to have a material financial effect upon 
his newspaper through the contract for publication of official 
notices. 
Presumably those decisions are made by other City 
officials and are pretty much ministerial in nature, being 
required by law in most circumstances. 
It is Mr. Miller's land 
use decisions which might have an effect on his newspaper or 
upon sources of income to him through the newspaper. 
In each 
instance, the analysis is the same: 
(1) 
Is there a f1nanc1al interest present unaer 
Section 87103? 
(2) 
Is it reasonably foreseeable that the decision 
will have a material financial effect upon that interest? 
(3) 
Will that effect be distinguishable from the 
decision's effect upon the public generally? 
If each part of the analysis is answered affirmatively, then 
Mr. Miller must disqualify himself unless his participation is 
legally required under Section 87101 and Commission regulation, 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18701. 
I trust that this has satisfactorily responded to your 
question. 
Should you desire further clarification, please do 
not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
REL:plh 
IRobert E. ~&eidign 
Counsel 
Legal Division 
~/ See Section 82030(a), which provides that where an 
official has a 
~O% or greater ownership interest in a business 
entity he is considered to receive a pro rata share of income 
form any source of 1ncome to the business entity. 
Where 
ownership is 100%, the full amount of income from any source is 
attributable to the official. 


3 
Robert E. Leidigh, Esq. 
Legal Division 
3 
May 26, 1983 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
Post Office Box 807 
Sacramento, California 
95804 
Dear Mr. Leidigh:

One of our Planning Commissioners, Mr. Jack Miller, 
recently purchased a local newspaper that has a contract 
with the City for the publication of the City's legal 
notices, including notices of hearings before the Planning 
Commision and other commissions and committees of the City, 
as well as meetings of the City Council. 
The question has arisen as to whether or not this 
creates a conflict of interest for the Planning Commis-
sioner. 
I have been instructed by the City Council to 
obtain your written opinion concerning this situtation. 
If 
you need any additional information, please let me know. 
Thank you for your attention to this matter. 
Very truly yours, 
PHM/np

One of our Planning Commissioners, Mr. Jack Miller, 
recently purchased a local newspaper that has a contract 
with the City for the publication of the City's legal 
notices, including notices of hearings before the Planning 
Commision and other commissions and committees of the City,

sections.conclusion: Mr. Miller may not make or participate in making any decisions which will materially affect such financial interests in a manner which is distinguishable from the decisions' effects on the public generally. Mr. Miller's decisions as a planning commissioner are not likely to be such as to have a material financial effect upon his newspaper through the contract for publication of official notices. It is Mr. Miller's land use decisions which might have an effect on his newspaper or upon sources of income to him through the newspaper. In each instance, Mr. Miller must disqualify himself unless his participation is legally required under Section 87101 and Commission regulation, if the three-part test is answered affirmatively: (1) Is there a financial interest present under Section 87103? (2) Is it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect upon that interest? (3) Will that effect be distinguishable from the decision's effect upon the public generally?
sections.question_synthetic: Does a planning commissioner's recent purchase of a local newspaper that has a contract with the city to publish official notices create a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A planning commissioner with a financial interest in a newspaper holding a city contract must disqualify himself from decisions that could materially affect that financial interest in a manner distinguishable from effects on the public generally. The publication contract itself is unlikely to create a conflict, but land use decisions could potentially trigger the conflict of interest rules depending on a three-part analysis.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request (dated May 26, 1983) and the FPPC response (dated June 8, 1983). OCR quality is generally good. The letter cites Government Code sections and provides detailed legal analysis with statutory framework.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to inquiry about whether a planning commissioner's ownership of a local newspaper with a city contract creates a conflict of interest. The letter applies the Political Reform Act's conflict of interest provisions and provides a three-part analytical framework for determining when disqualification is required.
